name: Tao Te Ching
description: Lao Tzu on the Way, simplicity, and the art of effortless action.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: The Tao that can be trodden is not the enduring and unchanging Tao. The name that can be named is not the enduring and unchanging name.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 1 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: The opening lines: the ultimate Way and its true name lie beyond words and fixed definition.

quotes.content: Therefore the sage manages affairs without doing anything, and conveys his instructions without the use of speech.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 2 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: An early statement of wu wei, action without forced effort, and teaching by example rather than words.

quotes.content: Heaven is long-enduring and earth continues long.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 7 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Heaven and earth endure because they do not live for themselves; the sage follows their selfless example.

quotes.content: The highest excellence is like (that of) water.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 8 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: The famous water simile: the greatest good yields and flows rather than contends. Parentheses are Legge's own.

quotes.content: The excellence of water appears in its benefiting all things, and in its occupying, without striving (to the contrary), the low place which all men dislike.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 8 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Water models the Way by nourishing everything and settling into the lowly places others avoid.

quotes.content: It is better to leave a vessel unfilled, than to attempt to carry it when it is full.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 9 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: A caution against excess and overreaching; knowing when to stop preserves what one has.

quotes.content: When gold and jade fill the hall, their possessor cannot keep them safe.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 9 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Hoarded riches breed anxiety and cannot be secured; the chapter counsels withdrawal once the work is done.

quotes.content: In the highest antiquity, (the people) did not know that there were (their rulers). In the next age they loved them and praised them.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 17 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: The best rulers govern so lightly that subjects barely notice them; lesser ones are merely loved, feared, or despised.

quotes.content: We are as we are, of ourselves!
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 17 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: When the ideal ruler's work succeeds, the people say this, feeling they did it themselves; a model of effortless leadership.

quotes.content: The partial becomes complete; the crooked, straight; the empty, full; the worn out, new.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 22 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: The paradox of yielding: bending and emptiness are the conditions for wholeness and renewal.

quotes.content: He who displays himself does not shine; he who asserts his own views is not distinguished.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 24 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Self-promotion defeats itself; the Taoist ideal is quiet self-effacement rather than striving for recognition.

quotes.content: He who knows other men is discerning; he who knows himself is intelligent.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 33 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Knowing others is cleverness, but true insight is self-knowledge; often paraphrased as knowing yourself is enlightenment.

quotes.content: He who overcomes others is strong; he who overcomes himself is mighty.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 33 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Mastery of oneself is a greater power than dominance over others.

quotes.content: The Tao in its regular course does nothing (for the sake of doing it), and so there is nothing which it does not do.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 37 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: The core paradox of wu wei: the Way accomplishes everything precisely by not forcing anything. Parentheses are Legge's.

quotes.content: The softest thing in the world dashes against and overcomes the hardest.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 43 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Like water wearing away stone, gentleness and yielding prevail over rigid force.

quotes.content: Who is content / Needs fear no shame. / Who knows to stop / Incurs no blame.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 44 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Legge renders this chapter as verse; contentment and knowing when to stop are the safeguards against loss. Slashes mark his line breaks.

quotes.content: There is no guilt greater than to sanction ambition; no calamity greater than to be discontented with one's lot.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 46 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Discontent and unchecked desire are named the root of misfortune; the chapter closes praising the contentment of enough.

quotes.content: He who devotes himself to learning (seeks) from day to day to increase (his knowledge).
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 48 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Contrasts accumulating learning with the pursuit of the Tao, which instead subtracts until one reaches non-action. Parentheses are Legge's.

quotes.content: Having arrived at this point of non-action, there is nothing which he does not do.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 48 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: The fruit of wu wei: by reducing effort to non-action, everything is accomplished.

quotes.content: He who knows (the Tao) does not (care to) speak (about it).
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 56 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Those who truly understand the Way say little; those who talk most understand least. Parentheses are Legge's.

quotes.content: All difficult things in the world are sure to arise from a previous state in which they were easy.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 63 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Great difficulties grow from small easy beginnings, so the wise act early while matters are still manageable.

quotes.content: Therefore the sage sees difficulty even in what seems easy, and so never has any difficulties.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 63 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: By respecting small tasks before they grow hard, the sage avoids being overwhelmed.

quotes.content: The tree which fills the arms grew from the tiniest sprout; the tower of nine storeys rose from a (small) heap of earth; the journey of a thousand li commenced with a single step.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 64 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Legge's exact wording of the famous 'thousand-mile journey' passage; great things grow from small beginnings. Parentheses are Legge's.

quotes.content: He who acts (with an ulterior purpose) does harm; he who takes hold of a thing (in the same way) loses his hold.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 64 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Grasping and scheming defeat their own ends; the sage acts without forcing and so loses nothing. Parentheses are Legge's.

quotes.content: Because he does not strive, no one finds it possible to strive with him.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 66 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Like rivers and seas that lead by lying low, the sage rises above others by not contending.

quotes.content: Man at his birth is supple and weak; at his death, firm and strong.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 76 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Suppleness belongs to the living and rigidity to the dead; softness and flexibility are aligned with life.

quotes.content: There is nothing in the world more soft and weak than water.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 78 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: Yet nothing surpasses water for wearing down the hard and strong, the chapter's emblem of yielding power.

quotes.content: Every one in the world knows that the soft overcomes the hard, and the weak the strong.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 78 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: A truth all recognize yet few can practice; the weak and yielding ultimately prevail.

quotes.content: Sincere words are not fine; fine words are not sincere.
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 81 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: The closing chapter contrasts plain truth with ornamented speech; honesty needs no embellishment.

quotes.content: The sage does not accumulate (for himself).
quotes.source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 81 (James Legge translation)
quotes.notes: The book ends on generosity: the more the sage gives to others, the more he himself has. Parentheses are Legge's.
